title: Enhancing Many-Body Chaos via Entropy Injection from Environment
authors: Yuke Zhang, Wenbo Zhou, Pengfei Zhang
abstract: In closed quantum systems, local information spreads throughout the entire system and becomes highly complex under unitary evolution. In contrast, when the system is embedded in an environment, system-environment coupling can transfer information from the system into the environment, thereby reducing the rate of complexity growth within the system. This leads to the environment-induced scrambling transition established in previous works. In this work, we identify entropy injection from the environment as a different physical process that instead enhances many-body chaos. Our setup consists of coupling a system that is already in equilibrium with one environment to another environment, which serves as an entropy reservoir and drives the system into a non-equilibrium state. When entropy flows into the system through either heat transfer or particle transfer, the effective Hilbert space explored by the system enlarges, a mechanism that can enhance many-body chaos. We explicitly demonstrate this idea by constructing a solvable complex Brownian SYK model, in which both the relaxation toward the steady state and the steady-state quantum Lyapunov exponent can be computed analytically. Our results provide a controllable mechanism for tuning quantum scrambling through entropy flow in quantum many-body systems coupled to environments.
summary: The paper explores how coupling a quantum system to an environment can drive it into a non-equilibrium state by injecting entropy. By analyzing the complex Brownian SYK model, the authors demonstrate that this process enhances many-body chaos, providing a tunable mechanism for controlling quantum scrambling beyond simple dissipation effects.
main_problem: To investigate entropy injection from an environment as a mechanism that enhances many-body chaos, contrasting with previous work suggesting environmental coupling can reduce complexity growth.
main_result: Entropy injection into the system enlarges the effective Hilbert space dimension, which demonstrably enhances the quantum Lyapunov exponent and promotes many-body chaos in the solvable complex Brownian SYK model.
method: The analysis uses large-N expansion techniques on Green's functions governed by a Kadanoff-Baym equation to derive an exact quantum Boltzmann equation for particle number evolution and calculate OTOCs.
model_or_system: The system is modeled as complex fermions coupled sequentially to two environments ($E_1$ and $E_2$), realized via a solvable complex Brownian Sachdev-Ye-Kitaev (SYK) model. The coupling drives the system into a non-equilibrium steady state.
key_observables: Quantum Lyapunov exponent ($\lambda$ or $\kappa$), Out-of-Time-Ordered Correlators (OTOCs), and the accessible effective Hilbert space dimension ($e_S$).
important_parameters: Coupling strengths ($\gamma_1, \gamma_2$) and environment parameters ($n, m$), which define different dynamical regimes.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on large-N expansion techniques and the use of specific coupling structures (e.g., pair-hopping) to maintain solvability.
figures_summary: Figures show contour plots of the quantum Lyapunov exponent across parameter space for both standard and modified couplings, illustrating phase boundaries between scrambling and dissipative regimes.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical setup (entropy injection vs. dissipation), deriving the governing equations (Boltzmann/Kadanoff-Baym), calculating key diagnostics like OTOCs and $\lambda$, and finally comparing results across different coupling mechanisms to establish control over chaos enhancement.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete, controllable mechanism—tuning entropy flow—to actively enhance quantum chaos in many-body systems, which is highly relevant for understanding non-equilibrium dynamics far from thermal equilibrium.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of the setup. Filled and empty circles represent occupied and unoccupied modes, respec- tively, with J denoting the intra-system coupling strength. For t < 0, the system is coupled to a single bath with strength V . At t = 0, a second bath is activated with coupling strength κ, inducing a change in the system’s density accompanied by entropy injection. At later times (t > 0), the system relaxes into a steady state, where the quantum Lyapunov exponent is evaluated as a signature of the quantum butterfly effect.

FIG. 2. Contour plots of the quantum Lyapunov exponent for the complex Brownian SYK model with the original SE2 coupling (3), for (a) m = 0.1, (b) m = 0.2, (c) m = 0.48, and (d) m = 0.8. We fix n = 0.2 and J = 1 in all panels. The dashed lines denote the phase boundary between the scram- bling phase and the dissipative phase.

FIG. 3. Contour plots of the quantum Lyapunov exponent for the complex Brownian SYK model with the pair-hopping coupling (13), for (a) m = 0.1, (b) m = 0.2, (c) m = 0.48, and (d) m = 0.8. We fix n = 0.2 and J = 1 in all panels. The dashed lines denote the phase boundary between the scram- bling phase and the dissipative phase.
